subject: math
learning_objectives: Meaning of Simple and Compound Statements.
Logical Operations and the Truth Tables.

sections.heading: SIMPLE AND COMPOUND PROPOSITIONS
sections.content: A preposition is a statement or a sentence that is either true or false but not both. We shall use upper case letters of English alphabets such as A, B, C, D, P, Q, R, S, ‚Ä¶, to stand for simple statements or prepositions. A simple statement or proposition is a statement containing no connectives. In other words a proposition is considered simple if it cannot be broken up into sub-propositions. On the other hand, a compound proposition is made up of two or more propositions joined by the connectives. These connectives are and, or, if ‚Ä¶then, if and only if. They are also called logic operators. The table below shows the logic operators and their symbols. The following table: Logic Operator, Symbol And, ‚ãÄ or, ‚ãÅ if ‚Ä¶ then, ‚áí if and only if, ‚áî Not, ~ The statement ~ P is known as the negation of P. thus ~ P means not P or ‚Äòit is false that P‚Ä¶‚Äô or ‚Äòit is not true that P‚Ä¶‚Äô If P and Q are two statements (or propositions), then: The statement P ‚ãÄ Q is called the conjunction of P and Q. thus, P ‚ãÄ Q means P and Q. The statement P ‚ãÅ Q is called the disjunction of P and Q. thus, P ‚ãÅ Q means either P or Q or both P and Q. notice that the inclusive or is used. The statement P ‚áí Q is called the conditional of P and Q. a conditional is also known as implication P ‚áí Q means if P then Q or P implies Q. The statement P ‚áî Q is called the biconditional of P and Q, where the symbol ‚áî means if and only if (or iff for short). Thus P ‚áî Q means P ‚áí Q and Q ‚áí P. [cite: 838, 854]

sections.heading: The Truth Tables
sections.content: The truth or falsity of a proposition is its truth value, ie. A proposition that is true has a truth value T and a proposition that is false has a truth value F. the truth tables for the logical operators are given below. Figure 2 The following table: P,~ P T,F F,T If P is true (T), then ~ P is false and if P is false, then ~ P is true. Recall that other symbols used instead of ~ are P‚Äô or P or ~ P. Figure 3 figure 4 The following table: P,Q,P ‚ãÄ Q T,T,T T,F,F F,T,F F ,F,F The following table: P,Q,PQ ‚ãÅ T,T,T T,F,T F,T,T F,F,F P ‚ãÄ Q is true when both P ‚ãÅ Q is false when both P and Q are false. P and Q are true The following table: P,Q,P ‚áî Q T,T,T T,F,F F,T,F F,F,T Figure 6 Figure 5 The following table: P,Q,P ‚áí Q T,T,T T,F,F F,T,T F,F,T P Q is false when P is P Q is true when both P and Q are true and Q is false either both true or both false. Example 1 Translate the following into symbols and then determine which statements are true or false. -5 < 8 and 2 < - 50 4 right angles = 360o or opposite angles of any quadrilateral and supplementary. If a person is 20 years old, then the person is a teenager. 2x ‚Äì 5 = 9 if and only if x = 7. Solution Let P = (-5 < 8); Q = (2 < -50) P - -5 < 8 is true (T) Q = 2 < -50 is false (F) ‚à¥ symbolic for: P ‚ãÄ Q is false (see 2nd row of fig 3) Let P = (4 right angles = 360o) Q (opposite angles of any quadrilateral are supplementary). P is true (T) and Q is false (F) ‚à¥ P ‚ãÅ Q is true (see 2nd row of fig 4) Let P = a person is 20 years old. Q = a person is a teenager. P is T and Q is F ‚à¥ P ‚áí Q is false (see 2nd row of fig 5) 2x ‚Äì 5 = 9 if and only if x = 7 Let P = (2x ‚Äì 5 = 9) and Q = (x = 7) When x = 7, 2x ‚Äì 5 = 2 X 7 ‚Äì 5 = 14 ‚Äì 5 = 9 (T) Both P and W have the same T values. ‚à¥ P ‚ü∫ Q is true (see 1st row of fig 6) [cite: 855, 898]

sections.heading: Converse, Inverse and Contrapositive of Conditional Statement
sections.content: Converse statement The converse of the conditional statement ‚Äòif P then Q‚Äô is the conditional statement ‚Äòif Q then P‚Äô, i.e. the converse of P ‚áí Q is Q ‚áí P. Inverse statement The inverse of the conditional statement ‚Äòif P then Q‚Äô is the conditional statement ‚Äòif not P then not Q‚Äô. i.e. the inverse of P ‚áí Q is ‚àº P ‚áí‚àº Q. Contrapositive statement The converse of the conditional statement ‚Äòif P then Q‚Äô is the conditional statement ‚Äòif not Q then not P‚Äô. i.e. the contrapositive of P ‚áí Q is ‚àº P ‚áí‚àºP . Example Give the (a) converse (b) inverse (c) contrapositive of the following: (i) If 9 < 19, then 8 < 5 + 6. (ii) if two triangles are equiangular, then their corresponding sides are proportional. Solution (i) if 8 < 5 + 6 ‚áí 9 < 19. (ii) If two triangles have their corresponding sides proportional, then they are equiangular. (i) if 9 ‚âÆ 19 ‚áí 8 ‚âÆ 5 + 6. (ii) If two triangles are not equiangular, then their corresponding sides are not proportional (i) if ‚âÆ 8 + 6 ‚áí 9 ‚âÆ 19 (ii) if two triangles do not have their corresponding sides proportional, then they are not equiangular [cite: 899, 919]

sections.heading: LOGICAL OPERATIONS AND TRUTH TABLES
sections.content: Example Construct the truth tables for the following: ( ‚àº P ‚ãÅ‚àº Q) ‚áí (P ‚ãÄ‚àº Q) Solution Method 1 The following table: P,Q,‚àºP,‚àºQ,‚àºP ‚ãÅ ‚àºQ,P ‚ãÄ‚àºQ,( ‚àº P ‚ãÅ‚àº Q) ‚áí (P ‚ãÄ‚àº Q) T,T,F,F,F,F,T T,F,F,T,T,T,T F,T,T,F,T,F,F F,F,T,T,T,F,F Explanation Since there are two variables, P and Q, we will have 4 rows. P column: Enter two T‚Äôs, then two F‚Äôs Q column: Enter one T, then one F. ‚àº P column: Enter the negation of P. ‚àº Q column: Enter the negation of Q. Fill in the truth values of ‚àºP ‚ãÅ ‚àºQ . ‚àºP ‚ãÅ ‚àºQ is false when both ‚àº P and ‚àºQ are false according to the table for V. Fill in the truth values of P ‚ãÄ‚àº Q is true when both P and ‚àº Q are true. Fill in the truth value of ( ‚àº P V ‚àºQ ) ‚áí (P V ‚àº Q) ( ‚àº P V ‚àºQ ) ‚áí (P V ‚àº Q) is false when ( ‚àº P V ‚àºQ ) is true and (P V ‚àºQ ) is false. Method 2 The following table: P,Q,‚àºP,‚àºQ,( ‚àº P ‚ãÅ‚àº Q) ‚áí (P ‚ãÄ‚àº Q) T,T,F,F,F,T,F T,F,F,T,T,T,T F,T,T,F,T,F,F F,F,T,T,T,F,F (1) (2) (3) (4) (5) Explanation Enter P and Q columns as usual. fill in the truth values of ‚àºP. Fill in the truth values of ‚àº Q. Fill in the truth values of ( ‚àº P ‚ãÄ‚àº Q) Fill in the truth values of (P ‚ãÄ‚àº Q). Now consider the implication ( ‚áí ) as a whole. Note : The columns of the truth table are completed in the indicated order Tautology and Contradiction When a compound proposition is always true for every combination of values of its constituent statements, it is called a tautology . On the other hand, when the proposition is always false it is called a contradiction. Example Construct the truth tables to show that: (a) P ‚áî‚àº ( ‚àº P) is a tautology (b) (P ‚ãÄ Q) ‚áî [( ‚àº P) V ( ‚àº Q)] is a contradiction. Solution The following table: P,~ P,~ ( ~ P),P ‚ü∫~ ( ~ P) T,F,T,T F,T,F,T The truth table of P ‚áî~ ( ~ P ) i s always T, so it is a tautology The following table: P,Q,"(P ‚ãÄ Q) ‚áî [( ~ P) V( ~ Q)]" T,T,T,F,F,F,F T,F,F,F,F,T,T F,T,F,F,T,T,F T,F,F,F,T,T,T (1) (5) (2) (4) (3) Column (5) shows that the truth table of (P ‚ãÄ Q) ‚áî [( ~ P) V ( ~ Q)] is always F, so it is a contradiction. Example Find the truth values of the following when the variables P, Q and R are all true. (a) ~ P ‚ãÄ~ Q (b) ~ (P ‚ãÄ~ Q) V ~ R Solution ~ P ‚ãÄ~ Q Substituting the truth values directly into the statement ~ P ‚ãÄ~ Q, we have ~ T ‚ãÄ~ T. But ~ T is the same as F. ‚à¥ ~ T ‚ãÄ~ T gives F ‚ãÄ F Simplify the disjunction: F ‚à¥ The compound statement ~ P ‚ãÄ~ Q is false. ~ (P ‚ãÄ~ Q) V ~ R Substituting the truth values: ~ (T ‚ãÄ~ T) V ~ T Within brackets, negate: ~ (T ‚ãÄ F) V ~ T Simplify brackets: ~ F V ~ T T V F Simplify disjunction: T ‚à¥ ~ (P ‚ãÄ~ Q) V ~ R is true. [cite: 920, 979]

sections.heading: THE CHAIN RULE
sections.content: The chain rule states that if X, Y and Z are statements such that X ‚áí Y and Y ‚áí Z, then X ‚áí Z. a chain of statements can have as many ‚Äòlinks‚Äô as necessary. Example 5 is an example of the chain rule. When using chain rule. It is essential that the implication arrows point in the same direction. It is not of much value, for example, to have something like X ‚áí Q ‚áê R because no useful deductions can be made from it. Example In the following argument, determine whether or not the conclusion necessarily follows from the given premises. All drivers are careful. (1st premise) Careful people are patient (2nd premise) Therefore all drivers are patient (conclusion) If D: people who are drivers C: people who are careful P: people who are patient Then D ‚áí C (1st premise) And C ‚áí P (2nd premise) If D ‚áí C and C ‚áí P Then D ‚áí P (chain rule) The conclusion follows from the premises. Example Determine the validity of each of the proposed conclusions if the premises of an argument are X: Teachers are contented people. Y: Every doctor is rich Z: No one who is contented is also rich. Proposed conclusions S1: No teacher is rich S2: Doctors are contented people S3: No one can be both a teacher and a doctor. Let C = {contented people} T = {teachers} D = {doctors} R = {rich people} The figure below is a Venn diagram for the premises. T D C R From the figure, the following conclusions can be deduced. i. S1 is true, i.e. no teacher is rich. (T ‚ãÇ R = ‚àÖ ) ii. S2 is false, i.e. doctors are contented people is false. (D ‚ãÇ C = ‚àÖ ) iii. S3 is true, i.e. no one can be a teacher and a doctor. (T ‚ãÇ D = ‚àÖ ) [cite: 1009, 1047]

sections.heading: CONDITIONAL STATEMENTS AND INDIRECT PROOFS.
sections.content: Another method we can use to determine the validity of arguments especially the more complex ones is to construct the truth tables as will be seen in the following examples. Example 1 Write the argument below symbolically and determine whether the argument is valid. 1st premise: if tortoises eat well, then they live long 2nd premise: Tortoises eat well. Conclusion: Tortoises live long. Solution To determine the truth value, the steps are: Write the arguments in symbolic forms. Let P = ‚Äòtortoises eat well‚Äô Q = ‚Äòthey live long‚Äô. 1st premise becomes P ‚áí Q. 2nd premise is P and the conclusion is Q. ‚à¥ the argument is written as follows: P ‚áí Q (if P happens, then Q will happen) PQ (P happens) (Q happens) From the conjunction of the two premises. (P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ P Let the conjunction in (2) implies the conclusion Q. i.e. [(P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ P] ‚áí Q The following table: P,Q,P ‚áí Q,(P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ p,"[(P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ P] ‚áí Q" T,T,T,T,T T,F,F,F,T F,T,T,F,T F,F,T,F,T Since the compound statement [(P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ P] ‚áí Q is always a tautology, (i.e. has a truth value T), the argument is valid. This type of argument is called direct reasoning or modus ponems Example 2 Determine whether the following argument is valid. If you study this book, then you will pass WAEC. If you pass WAEC, then you will go to university Therefore, if you study this book, then you will to go university. Solution Let P: you study this book Q: you will pass WAEC. R: you will go to university. If you study this book, then you will pass WAEC becomes P ‚áí Q. If you pass WAEC, then you will go to university becomes Q ‚áí R. Therefore, if you study this book, then you will go to university becomes P ‚áí R. The above may be written as follows: 1st premise: P ‚áí Q 2nd premise: Q ‚áí R Conclusion: P ‚áí R From the conjunction of the premises as (P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ (Q ‚áí R) Let the conjunction implies the conclusion implies the conclusion. i.e. [(P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ (Q ‚áí R)] ‚áí (P ‚áí R) P Q R [(P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ (Q ‚áí R)] ‚áí (P ‚áí R) T T T T TTTT T T F T F F T F T F T F F T TT T F F F F T T F F T T T TTTT F T F T F F T T F F T T TTTT F F F T TTTT (1) (3) (2) (5) (4) Column (5) shows that the compound statement [(P ‚áí Q) ‚ãÄ (Q ‚áí R)] ‚áí (P ‚áí R) is always tautology. Therefore, the argument is valid. This type of argument is called transitive reasoning or chain rule or the rule of syllogism. Note: there are other forms of valid arguments which you can investigate on your own. [cite: 1048, 1103]
